section.title: Against Edom and the Mountains Restored
section.sectionRationale: Before the great promises of chapters 36-37 unfold, Ezekiel first turns against Edom, Israel's gloating kinsman-enemy who seized on Jerusalem's disaster to claim her land as spoil. Only once Edom's ancient hatred is judged does the LORD turn to comfort the very mountains of Israel that Edom mocked as desolate and abandoned, promising they will again be tilled, sown, and filled with returning people.
section.thesis: Edom's ancient hatred and gloating claim on Israel's land brings it the same desolation it wished on others, while the mountains of Israel it despised are promised fruitfulness, homecoming, and multiplied life, because the LORD's zeal for His own name and His land will not let either injustice or promise fail.
section.frameNote: Three movements: judgment pronounced against Mount Seir for its ancient hatred and bloodshed; Edom's contemptuous boast against the mountains of Israel turned back on its own head; and the LORD's burning zeal to restore and repopulate the very mountains the nations mocked as desolate.

units.label: Against Mount Seir
units.note: Edom judged for an ancient hatred and bloodshed

verses.text: Moreover, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, set your face against Mount Seir and prophesy against it,

and declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: 
Behold, I am against you, O Mount Seir. I will stretch out My hand against you and make you a desolate waste.

I will turn your cities into ruins, and you will become a desolation. 
Then you will know that I am the LORD.
Because you harbored an {{eybah_olam|ancient hatred}} and delivered the Israelites over to the sword in the time of their disaster at the final stage of their punishment,
therefore as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will give you over to bloodshed and it will pursue you. Since you did not hate bloodshed, it will pursue you.
I will make Mount Seir a desolate waste and will cut off from it those who come and go.
I will fill its mountains with the slain; those killed by the sword will fall on your hills, in your valleys, and in all your ravines.
I will make you a perpetual desolation, and your cities will not be inhabited. Then you will know that I am the LORD.

units.label: Edom's boast against the mountains of Israel

verses.text: Because you have said, ‘These two nations and countries will be ours, and we will possess them,’ even though the LORD was there,

therefore as surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will treat you according to the anger and jealousy you showed in your hatred against them, and I will make Myself known among them when I judge you.
Then you will know that I, the LORD, have heard every contemptuous word you uttered against the mountains of Israel when you said, ‘They are desolate; they are given to us to devour!’
You boasted against Me with your mouth and multiplied your words against Me. I heard it Myself!
This is what the Lord GOD says: While the whole earth rejoices, I will make you desolate.

As you rejoiced when the inheritance of the house of Israel became desolate, so will I do to you. You will become a desolation, O Mount Seir, and so will all of Edom. Then they will know that I am the LORD.

units.label: The mountains of Israel restored
units.note: reproach reversed, home and fruitfulness promised

verses.text: “And you, son of man, prophesy to the mountains of Israel and say: O mountains of Israel, hear the word of the LORD.
This is what the Lord GOD says: Because the enemy has said of you, ‘Aha! The ancient heights have become our possession,’

therefore prophesy and declare that this is what the Lord GOD says: Because they have made you desolate and have trampled you on every side, so that you became a possession of the rest of the nations and were taken up in slander by the lips of their talkers,

therefore, O mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Lord GOD. 
This is what the Lord GOD says to the mountains and hills, to the ravines and valleys, to the desolate ruins and abandoned cities, which have become a spoil and a mockery to the rest of the nations around you.

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Surely in My {{qinah|burning zeal}} I have spoken against the rest of the nations, and against all Edom, who took My land as their own possession with wholehearted joy and utter contempt, so that its pastureland became plunder.

Therefore, prophesy concerning the land of Israel and tell the mountains and hills, the ravines and valleys, that this is what the Lord GOD says: Behold, I have spoken in My burning zeal because you have endured the reproach of the nations.
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: I have sworn with an uplifted hand that surely the nations around you will endure reproach of their own.
But you, O mountains of Israel, will produce branches and bear fruit for My people Israel, for they will soon come home.
For behold, I am on your side; I will turn toward you, and you will be tilled and sown.
I will multiply the people upon you, the house of Israel in its entirety. The cities will be inhabited and the ruins rebuilt.

I will fill you with people and animals, and they will multiply and be fruitful. I will make you as inhabited as you once were, and I will make you prosper more than before. Then you will know that I am the LORD.
Yes, I will cause My people Israel to walk upon you; they will possess you, and you will be their {{nachalah|inheritance}}, and you will no longer deprive them of their children.
For this is what the Lord GOD says: Because people say to you, ‘You devour men and deprive your nation of its children,’
therefore you will no longer devour men or deprive your nation of its children, declares the Lord GOD.
I will no longer allow the taunts of the nations to be heard against you, and you will no longer endure the reproach of the peoples or cause your nation to stumble, declares the Lord GOD.”

eybah_olam.gloss: Ancient or perpetual hatred, built on the root eybah, enmity, the same word used of the hostility God sets between the serpent and the woman's seed in Genesis 3:15. Edom's grudge against its brother mirrors, in miniature, the deeper enmity running through Scripture's whole story.

qinah.gloss: Zeal or jealousy, the fierce, protective love and anger of a husband defending what belongs to him. Applied here to God's defense of His own land and name against those who mocked and plundered it.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance or possession, the land given to Israel as a lasting family portion. Edom claimed this inheritance for itself, but God insists it belongs to His people alone.

basic.context: Edom, descended from Esau, Israel's own brother, watched Jerusalem fall and moved in on the spoils, saying, 'These two nations and countries will be ours.' Because Edom nursed an ancient hatred and gloated over Israel's disaster rather than showing kinship, God pronounces Mount Seir a perpetual desolation. Then, in the same breath, He turns to comfort the very mountains of Israel the nations mocked as ruined, promising they will again be cultivated, resettled, and filled with His returning people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real, historical judgment on an actual neighboring nation for an actual betrayal (compare the entire book of Obadiah, written on the same theme), paired with a real historical promise of the land's agricultural and demographic restoration after the exile. Its abiding lesson is that gloating over another's downfall, especially a kinsman's, invites the same judgment, while God's zeal for His own name guarantees He will restore what enemies declared beyond hope.
basic.originalLanguages: Edom's crime is named an eyvat olam, an ancient hatred, built on the same root, eybah, enmity, used of the hostility God sets between the serpent and the woman's seed in Genesis 3:15. God's response is described as burning qin'ah, zeal or jealousy, the fierce, protective love a husband has for his wife, aimed here at defending His land's nachalah, its inheritance, from those who despised it.
basic.typology: Edom's judgment for gloating over a brother's disaster answers directly to Obadiah's oracle and anticipates final judgment on all who despise God's people and take pleasure in their downfall, Matthew 25:40-45. The mountains of Israel restored, tilled, and filled with people again anticipate creation itself, groaning under futility, being set free into the freedom of the glory of the children of God, Romans 8:19-21.
basic.christ: Edom stands for every proud enemy that gloats over God's people rather than showing mercy, and its judgment previews the final reckoning for all who do the same, Matthew 25:40-45. But the greater note here is restoration: God's zeal to bring dead land back to fruitful life is the same zeal that raises dry bones in the very next chapter and that, in Christ, reverses the curse on creation itself, Romans 8:19-23.
basic.god: God is zealous, fiercely protective of His own name and His own people, unwilling to let Edom's ancient hatred or the nations' contempt for Israel's ruined mountains go unanswered. He is equally zealous to restore what enemies have declared hopeless, promising fruitfulness and homecoming not because Israel deserves it but because His own reputation and covenant faithfulness are bound up in keeping His word.
basic.crossReferences: Obadiah 1:10-15 addresses the same crime of Edom's gloating and violence against its brother Jacob. Genesis 3:15 shares the same Hebrew root for hatred and enmity. Matthew 25:40-45 shows Jesus applying the same principle to how nations treat His brothers. Romans 8:19-21 previews creation's own coming restoration, foreshadowed here.
basic.oneStory: This is the reckoning before the restoration: Edom's ancient hatred and gloating over Israel's fall is judged, and the very mountains it mocked as desolate are promised fruitfulness and homecoming instead. God's burning zeal for His name will not let contempt for His people or His land have the last word, a pattern that runs all the way to the new creation, where every desolate place is finally, fully restored.

context.literary: A matched pair of oracles: judgment against Mount Seir (35:1-15), then, in deliberate contrast, comfort spoken directly to the mountains of Israel (36:1-15), the same geographic image, mountains, used to condemn one nation and console another.
context.historical: Edom exploited Jerusalem's fall to seize territory and gloat over Judah's disaster, a betrayal by a kindred nation (descended from Esau) that provoked the entire prophecy of Obadiah as well as this oracle; the mountains of Israel had in the meantime become a byword for ruin among the surrounding nations.
context.canonical: Connects to the wider prophetic tradition of judgment on Edom for betraying its brother (Obadiah, Amos 1:11-12, Malachi 1:2-5) and anticipates the fuller land-restoration promises of Ezekiel 36:16-38 and 37, which this passage sets up.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To judge Edom for its ancient hatred and opportunistic gloating over Jerusalem's fall, and to comfort the mountains of Israel, reversing the nations' mockery with a promise of renewed fruitfulness, population, and homecoming.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapters describe specific historical judgment on Edom and a specific historical promise of the land's restoration; they are not prescribing land policy. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that gloating over another's downfall invites judgment, and that God's zeal guarantees restoration for what He has promised to renew.

debates.question: Is Edom in these chapters purely the historical nation, or does it also function as a symbol for every hostile enemy of God's people?

views.label: Primarily the historical nation of Edom
views.case: The oracle names Mount Seir and Edom specifically and responds to a documented historical betrayal at Jerusalem's fall, so most read it first as real judgment on a real, identifiable neighboring people.

views.label: Edom as a representative type of hostility to God's people
views.case: Other prophetic texts (Isaiah 34, Obadiah, Malachi 1:2-5) treat Edom almost as an archetype of enduring, brotherly hostility to God's covenant people, so some read this oracle as also establishing a pattern for how God judges every nation that gloats over His people's suffering, not Edom alone.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that gloating over God's people in their disaster invites real judgment, whether the offender is historical Edom specifically or any power that follows its example.

study.originalLanguages: Edom's crime is named an eyvat olam, an ancient hatred, built on the same root, eybah, enmity, used of the hostility God sets between the serpent and the woman's seed in Genesis 3:15; hatred between brothers here mirrors, in miniature, the deeper enmity running through the whole Bible's story. God's response is described as burning qin'ah, zeal or jealousy, the same fierce, protective love a husband has for his wife, aimed here at defending His land's nachalah, its inheritance, from those who despised it.

typology.type: Edom's judgment for gloating over a brother's disaster, Ezekiel 35:10-15
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates final judgment on all who despise and mistreat God's people, Matthew 25:40-45, Obadiah 1:15.

typology.type: The desolate mountains of Israel restored to fruitfulness and filled with people, Ezekiel 36:8-11
typology.fulfillment: Previews the whole creation's release from bondage to decay into the freedom of the glory of God's children, Romans 8:19-21, and the final undoing of all desolation, Revelation 21:1-5.

study.christ: Edom stands for every proud enemy that gloats over God's people rather than showing mercy, and its judgment previews the final reckoning for all who do the same, Matthew 25:40-45, Obadiah 1:15. But the greater note of this passage is restoration: God's zeal to bring dead land back to fruitful life is the same zeal that raises dry bones in the very next chapter and that, in Christ, reverses the curse on creation itself. Every barren mountain God repopulates here is a small preview of the far greater renewal Christ secures, where even creation's bondage to decay is undone, Romans 8:19-23.
study.god: God is zealous, fiercely protective of His own name and His own people, unwilling to let Edom's ancient hatred or the nations' contempt for Israel's ruined mountains go unanswered. He is equally zealous to restore what enemies have declared hopeless, promising fruitfulness and homecoming not because Israel deserves it but because His own reputation and covenant faithfulness are bound up in keeping His word.

crossReferences.note: Obadiah's entire short prophecy addresses the same crime: Edom's gloating and violence against its brother Jacob in the day of his disaster, judged by 'as you have done, it shall be done to you'.

The word for Edom's 'ancient hatred' (eybah) is the same word used for the enmity God sets between the serpent and the woman's seed, showing how deeply hostility between brothers echoes the larger enmity running through Scripture.

Jesus teaches that how the nations treat 'the least of these My brothers' is treated as done, or not done, to Him directly, the same principle underlying Edom's judgment for despising Israel's disaster.

Creation's own longing to be set free from bondage to decay is answered in miniature by the desolate mountains of Israel being restored to fruitfulness here, a preview of the whole creation's coming renewal.

study.oneStory: This is the reckoning before the restoration: Edom's ancient hatred and gloating over Israel's fall is judged, and the very mountains it mocked as desolate are promised fruitfulness and homecoming instead. God's burning zeal for His name will not let contempt for His people or His land have the last word, a pattern that runs all the way to the new creation, where every desolate place is finally, fully restored.

quiz.q: What specific charge does God bring against Mount Seir (Edom) in 35:5?

options.text: They harbored an ancient hatred and delivered the Israelites over to the sword in the time of their disaster.
options.note: Verse 5 states this exact charge, an ancient hatred acted on at Israel's moment of greatest need.

options.text: They refused to pay tribute to Israel's king.
options.note: No mention of tribute appears; the charge concerns hatred and violence, not taxation.

options.text: They built idol shrines on Israel's mountains.
options.note: The charge is betrayal and bloodshed against Israel in disaster, not idol construction.

options.text: They invaded Israel's territory with a standing army.
options.note: The charge is about delivering Israelites to the sword and opportunistic gloating, not a formal invasion.

quiz.q: What specific claim does Edom make about Israel's land in 35:10 and 36:2?

options.text: That these two nations and the ancient heights will become their own possession, even though the LORD was there.
options.note: Both verses record this exact claim of possession over Israel's territory.

options.text: That Israel had forfeited the land through an unfair treaty.
options.note: No treaty dispute is mentioned; the claim is a direct assertion of ownership by conquest opportunity.

options.text: That the land legally belonged to Assyria rather than Israel.
options.note: Edom claims the land for itself, not on behalf of Assyria.

options.text: That the land should be left uninhabited by anyone.
options.note: Edom explicitly wants to possess and devour the land, not leave it empty.

quiz.q: How does God answer Edom's gloating over Israel's disaster, 35:15?

options.text: As Edom rejoiced over Israel's ruin, so God will do to Edom, making it a desolation.
options.note: Verse 15 states this direct measure-for-measure judgment plainly.

options.text: God ignores Edom's words as unimportant.
options.note: The opposite is true; God says explicitly, 'I heard it Myself!' verse 13.

options.text: God rewards Edom's boldness with expanded territory.
options.note: God pronounces desolation on Edom, not reward, verse 15.

options.text: God asks Israel to make peace with Edom immediately.
options.note: No call to reconciliation appears here; the passage pronounces judgment on Edom.

quiz.q: What does God promise the mountains of Israel in 36:8-11?

options.text: They will produce branches and fruit for God's people, be tilled and sown, and be filled with multiplying people and animals.
options.note: Verses 8-11 detail exactly this fruitfulness, cultivation, and repopulation.

options.text: They will remain desolate forever as a permanent memorial.
options.note: The opposite is promised: full restoration to fruitfulness and habitation.

options.text: They will be given entirely to the surrounding nations to farm.
options.note: The mountains are promised to God's own people Israel, not to the surrounding nations, verse 8.

options.text: Nothing specific is promised to the mountains themselves.
options.note: Very specific promises are given directly to 'the mountains of Israel' by name throughout this section.

quiz.q: According to 36:5-6, why is God's judgment against Edom and the nations described as coming from His 'burning zeal'?

options.text: Because they took His land with wholehearted joy and utter contempt, making its pastureland their plunder.
options.note: Verse 5 states this cause explicitly as the reason for God's burning zeal.

options.text: Because they refused to worship at Jerusalem's temple.
options.note: The stated reason concerns contemptuous seizure of the land, not a worship dispute.

options.text: Because they were more militarily powerful than Israel.
options.note: Military strength is not the stated cause; contemptuous seizure of God's land is.

options.text: No reason is given for God's zeal in this passage.
options.note: A specific, clearly stated reason is given directly in verse 5.

questions: Edom's sin was gloating over a brother's disaster instead of showing mercy. Where might you be tempted to feel quiet satisfaction when someone who has wronged you or competed with you falls on hard times?

God calls Edom's hostility an 'ancient hatred', a grudge nursed over time rather than a single outburst. Is there a long-held resentment you are still nursing that this passage calls you to finally release?

God promises to restore mountains that the nations declared permanently desolate and beyond hope. What situation in your life feels like a 'desolate mountain' that you've stopped believing God could restore?

The whole point of God's zeal here is that His name and reputation are bound up with keeping His promises to His people. How does it change your confidence to know God's restoration of you is motivated by His own faithfulness, not by whether you've earned it?

Creation itself groans for the same kind of restoration promised to these mountains, Romans 8:19-21. Where do you most need to hold onto hope that God is still in the business of making desolate, hopeless places fruitful again?

translationNotes.note: 'Mount Seir' is the mountainous region south and east of the Dead Sea, the territory of Edom, so the name functions here as shorthand for the whole nation, much as 'Zion' can stand for Jerusalem or Israel.

'Ancient hatred' translates a Hebrew phrase (eyvat olam) built on the same root, eybah, rendered 'enmity' in Genesis 3:15, linking Edom's grudge against its brother to the deeper pattern of hostility running through Scripture's story.

'Burning zeal' (qin'ah) can also be rendered jealousy; the Hebrew word describes the fierce, protective love and anger of a husband defending what belongs to him, applied here to God's defense of His own land and name.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/Truth For Life, Keller/Gospel in Life, Piper/Desiring God, Ferguson, Carson/The Gospel Coalition) addressing Ezekiel 35:1-36:15 specifically was found via live web search this session, so sermon status is set to none rather than fabricated.
